ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for 'braised pork rice' as a combined dish; closest match is braised pork dish (FOODON_03311069). Rice component not captured in this single code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the braised pork component of this mixed rice dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated composite code for braised pork rice; A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the primary protein anchor. Rice base is separately classifiable under A00LC but not combined here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised pork rice as a composite dish; SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not cover this specific preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of braised pork rice is approximately 300 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with braised pork belly and sauce, as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Rice 卤肉饭 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010503 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, boiled (analogous estimate for rice-dominant mixed dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (boiled), GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The braised pork topping contributes fat and protein which may modestly lower the effective GI, but the rice-dominant composition justifies using white rice GI as the primary estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 75.57) / 100 ≈ 54.4 — however, using a conservative 300 g serving with 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g gives total carbohydrate of 75.57 g; GL = (72 × 75.57) / 100 = 54.4. Note: GL per serving is high at 54.4 for a full 300 g plate. The value 36.3 is calculated for a 200 g portion (typical rice component ~200 g): (72 × 25.19 × 2) / 100 = 36.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g from soy sauce-based braising liquid; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 693 mg sodium, representing 30–35% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced braising sauce poured over the rice, limit portion size to 200 g or less, and note that the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K : 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load but does not eliminate the risk at full serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 75.6 g total carbohydrate, producing a high glycaemic load that requires careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request brown rice substitution to lower the GI, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre moderates but does not eliminate the glycaemic impact of the white rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork belly is a fatty red meat with significant saturated fat content; regular consumption of braised pork belly is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk, compounded by the sodium load from soy sauce-based braising.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency to occasional rather than regular intake, request leaner pork cuts or smaller pork portions, reduce braising sauce to minimise sodium, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) and saturated fat, which amplify pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit braised pork belly due to its high arachidonic acid content; consider substituting with braised chicken breast or tofu, and request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Rice (卤肉饭, Lǔ Ròu Fàn) is a popular Chinese rice dish widely served at Singapore hawker centres and Taiwanese eateries. The dish consists of steamed white rice topped with slow-braised pork belly that has been cooked in a soy sauce-based braising liquid seasoned with five-spice powder, garlic, shallots, sugar, and rice wine. The pork belly becomes tender and richly flavoured through prolonged braising, and the reduced sauce is ladled over the rice to impart a savoury, slightly sweet umami flavour. The texture contrasts soft, yielding pork fat and lean meat against fluffy steamed rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The relatively high dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100 g) is notable for a rice-based dish and is attributed to the inclusion of braised vegetables and the pork-to-rice ratio. Clinically, the dish carries moderate concern for hypertension due to soy sauce sodium, moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to the high-GI white rice base, and moderate-to-high concern for cardiovascular disease due to pork belly saturated fat. It is not suitable for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is a staple lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese hawker culture and is culturally associated with Hokkien and Taiwanese culinary traditions.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95, review_status: reviewed). The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the analogous white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Confidence is set to high because the nutrient data is exact from an approved primary source; the GI estimation introduces a minor caveat documented in gi_source.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: HPB Singapore Foodo does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry (G00010503). Detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in the HPB database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source. Note that pork belly typically contains cholesterol; the 0.0 value may reflect the composite dish dilution effect with rice, or a reporting convention in the HPB database — consumers should be aware that the pork belly component does contain cholesterol. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice (boiled) GI ~72 from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for braised pork rice as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 6. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard hawker centre plate; actual serving sizes may vary between 250–400 g.
